title: What's in a U? The Shapes of Cognitive Development
abstract: “Little by little, the child develops, ” wrote an undergraduate in a friend’s cognitive development class, and so, for the most part, it is. In most respects, a 3-year-old is better than a 2-year-old, and a 2-year-old is better than a 1-year-old. Whether improvement is gradual or steep, it is rare to see children take a step backward. When children do take that rare step backward, researchers stand up and listen. The three target articles in this issue are a look at some of those rare moments. “U ” shaped progressions, in which good performance is followed by worse performance that is finally followed by better performance should remind us (if perhaps in reverse) of the famous riddle of the Sphinx: what at first walks on 4 legs, then 2, and finally 3? We all know that the answer to the Sphinx’s riddle is a human being, progressing from crawling to walking unassisted and finally, late in life, walking with the assistance of the “leg ” of a cane. But what explains the U’s of cognitive development? Namy, Campbell, and Tomasello (this issue) and Cashon and Cohen (this issue) take a standard approach to understanding U-shaped curves: as the product of a mix of different cognitive processes that come on-line at different points in development. Gershkoff-Stowe and Thelen (this issue) take a rather different view, suggesting that we should look outside the head, away from “programs ” and “modules” and out toward the body and the environment. Indeed, in one of the first case studies they discuss, Gershkoff-Stowe and Thelen present a rather convincing story. What at first steps unassisted, then doesn’t, and finally does? A human child, born with the mental machinery for guiding stepping but also with a metabolism that leads her to gain weight faster than she gains muscle mass. The newborn steps because she can. The 6-week-old doesn’t because she can’t; her legs just aren’t strong enough. Even-
